planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; bleeding heart goes summer-dormant in hot climates and should be sited where adjacent plants will fill the gap.

directions: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; bleeding heart is the quintessential spring shade perennial, producing graceful arching stems hung with pendulous, perfectly heart-shaped flowers in deep rose-pink with protruding white inner petals from mid-spring through early summer. The glaucous, dissected foliage is attractive in its own right. In cool climates it blooms for 6–8 weeks then fades gradually; in hot summer climates the plant goes fully dormant by midsummer — mark its location and plant shade-tolerant companions like hostas and ferns around it to cover the void. 'Gold Heart' has golden foliage in spring; 'Alba' is pure white-flowered; 'Valentine' has deep red flowers with white inner petals. All plant parts are toxic.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant pips (rhizome divisions) in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; lily of the valley spreads aggressively by rhizomes in suitable conditions and is best confined or planted where it can naturalize freely.

directions: Plant pips (root divisions) shallowly in fall or early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; lily of the valley is one of the most beloved and recognizable shade groundcovers in the world, producing two broad, ribbed leaves per stem and delicate sprays of tiny, perfectly bell-shaped, intensely and sweetly fragrant white flowers in late spring that are legendary in perfumery and bridal traditions. The plant spreads by underground rhizomes to form dense colonies that suppress weeds effectively. It goes dormant in summer; allow adequate space and companion plant to cover the bare ground. Thrives in cool, moist northern climates but performs poorly in the hot, humid deep South. All parts are extremely toxic — never confuse with wild garlic. 'Rosea' has pale pink flowers; 'Flore Pleno' has double flowers.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in spring; black mondo grass is slow-growing but extremely long-lived and virtually maintenance-free once established in suitable shaded conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in spring; black mondo grass is grown for its dramatically dark, almost jet-black, strap-shaped, arching evergreen leaves that form neat, slowly spreading clumps — the blackest foliage of any commonly grown hardy perennial. Small pale pink flowers appear in summer followed by shiny black berries, both relatively inconspicuous against the dark foliage. The plant grows slowly but is nearly indestructible once established, tolerating dry shade, clay, and considerable neglect. Invaluable for foliage contrast in shade gardens — the black leaves glow against chartreuse hostas, lime-green sedges, and silver-leaved plants. Excellent for path edging, container planting, and mass groundcover under trees.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted stem sections in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in spring; sweet woodruff naturalizes rapidly in cool, moist, shaded conditions and can become invasive in ideal habitat.

directions: Plant divisions or rooted stem pieces in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; sweet woodruff is a delicate-looking European native that forms a low, weed-suppressing carpet of bright, fresh-green whorled leaves topped with a froth of tiny, pure-white, four-petaled flowers in late spring that are classically used to flavor May wine (Maibowl). The fresh plant has a faint, pleasant hay-like scent; dried foliage intensifies to a strong, sweet, vanilla-like fragrance of coumarin used in perfumery and pot-pourri. It naturalizes beautifully as a groundcover under deciduous trees and in woodland gardens, spreading steadily by creeping stems without becoming overwhelming in average garden conditions, though it can run in moist, loose soils. Semi-evergreen in mild winters; dies to the ground in cold climates.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Transplant small clumps with intact rootball in moist, very acidic, humus-rich, cool soil in full shade in spring; bunchberry demands cold winters, cool summers, very acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5), and consistent moisture — it fails rapidly in warm, dry, or alkaline conditions.

directions: Transplant clumps with rootball intact in moist, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich, cool soil in full to partial shade in spring; bunchberry is a native boreal ground-layer plant that forms a carpet of tidy, four-leaved rosettes with four white bracts surrounding a central cluster of tiny flowers in late spring — structurally miniature versions of flowering dogwood flowers — followed by clusters of brilliant red berries in late summer that attract birds and are edible to humans (mild, mealy). It is one of the finest shade groundcovers in cold, acidic woodland gardens but is extremely particular about its requirements: cool summer temperatures, very acidic conditions, moist but not waterlogged soil, and at least a few months of hard winter cold. Spreads slowly by rhizomes in ideal conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; spiderwort self-sows freely and can become weedy in ideal conditions — deadhead promptly to prevent excessive seeding.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; common spiderwort is a native Eastern North American perennial that produces clusters of striking three-petaled flowers in intense purple-blue, pink, or white from late spring through early summer, with each flower lasting only a single day but continuously replaced from clustered buds. The long, lance-shaped, folded leaves are distinctly grass-like. After the bloom period in early summer, cut the entire plant back by half to two-thirds — it regrows within weeks with fresh, attractive foliage and often a second flush of bloom in fall. Tolerates a wide range of conditions including clay soil, seasonal wet feet, and partial shade. Valuable early-season nectar source for bees. Native ecotype selections include 'Zwanenburg Blue', 'Purple Dome', and 'Osprey' (white).
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
